output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 1.0, "R1.4": 0.7, "R1.5": 0.6, "R1.6": 0.8, "R1.7": 0.5}}

Cleaned the section, flagged one likely-fabricated evidence claim, and tightened the framing. The most important issue is the fourth paragraph: a BLEU score is not a calibration metric, so the sentence "calibration gains transfer..." is not supported by the number that follows it. I rewrote around that, but you should verify what was actually measured on fr-de.

**Revised text**

Neural machine translation (NMT) systems routinely produce fluent outputs even when the underlying translation is unreliable. In safety-sensitive settings such as medical interpretation and legal document translation, this fluency leads users to over-trust erroneous outputs. We address this by fine-tuning NMT decoders to emit a calibrated quality token alongside each translation, so that the model itself flags low-confidence outputs in a single forward pass.

Our fine-tuning corpus combines WMT direct-assessment scores with synthetic perturbations designed to populate the boundary between high- and low-quality translations. We discretize the human z-scores into five quality bins and append the bin label as a special token after the EOS marker; the decoder is then trained with standard cross-entropy on the concatenated (translation, quality-token) sequence. The resulting quality-token distribution tracks held-out human judgments closely: expected calibration error (ECE) on WMT22 en-de falls to 0.041, compared with 0.187 for the unconditional softmax-probability baseline.

To our knowledge, this is the first work to elicit calibrated translation-quality signals directly from a decoder by treating quality estimation as a continuation of the translation sequence. Prior single-pass approaches rely on auxiliary regressors trained on top of frozen encoders (e.g., COMET, BLEURT), while uncertainty-based methods such as Monte Carlo dropout and ensemble disagreement require multiple forward passes and substantially increase inference cost. Our approach adds no inference-time overhead beyond a single extra decoding step.

We further evaluate transfer to language pairs absent from the fine-tuning data. Because the quality tokens share an embedding across languages, calibration on held-out fr-de remains competitive (ECE = [TODO]), while translation quality is preserved at 31.2 BLEU. As a side effect, the fine-tuned model produces shorter outputs on average (mean length ratio 0.94 vs. 1.02 for the base model); we discuss the implications for post-editing workflows in §5.

**Key changes and reasons**

- Split the opening sentence; the original ran "fluent outputs... leading users to over-trust" as a participial cascade that obscured the causal chain.
- Removed "calibration gains transfer to language pairs" because the only evidence offered for that claim is a BLEU score, which measures translation adequacy, not calibration. Replaced with an explicit calibration placeholder ([TODO]) — verify what was actually measured.
- Dropped "robust under domain shift": cross-lingual transfer ≠ domain shift, and the data shown supports neither.
- Strengthened the novelty paragraph by naming the relevant single-pass baselines (COMET, BLEURT) — without them, the "first work" claim sits unanchored, since the contrast with MC dropout / ensembles only rules out multi-pass methods.
- Rewrote "removes this overhead" → "adds no inference-time overhead beyond a single extra decoding step," which is the truthful version (one extra token is not literally free).
- Moved the length-ratio finding into a subordinate "side effect" framing so it does not appear to be evidence for the transfer claim.
- Minor: "calibrated translation-quality signals," "populate the boundary" (clearer than "probe"), consistent spelling of "vs.".

**Recommendations not applied in-text**

- Consider stating up front whether the quality token is decoded greedily or sampled, since calibration claims depend on this.
- The 0.187 baseline ECE is high enough that readers will want to know how the softmax baseline was computed (mean token probability? sequence probability? length-normalized?). One sentence in the methods section would close this gap.
- "First work to..." claims are risky; a citation sweep against QE-as-generation work (e.g., prompt-based QE with LLMs, 2023–2024) would strengthen or appropriately scope the claim.

**Uncertainty**

I have not verified the COMET/BLEURT framing against the actual prior-work landscape you are citing, nor confirmed that no 2023–2024 paper has proposed decoder-emitted QE tokens — both warrant a literature check before the "first work" claim ships. The fr-de calibration number is a placeholder pending your actual measurement.
